Substitute the series into a differential equation for dr/dθ. Neglect all O ( µ 2 ) terms, and thereby derive a simple differential equation for r 1 ( θ ). Solve this equation explicitly for r 1 ( θ ). (The approximation technique used here is called regular perturbation theory; see Section 7.6. ) b)  Find the maximum and minimum r on your approximate orbit, and hence show that it lies in the annulus 1 − μ < r < 1 + μ , as expected. c)  Use a computer to calculate r ( θ ) numerically for various small µ , and plot the results on the same graph as your analytical approximation for r ( θ ). How does the maximum error depend on µ? 7.3.10 Consider the two-dimensional system ẋ = A x − r 2 x, where r = ||x|| and A is a 2 × 2 constant real matrix with complex eigenvalues α ± iω. Prove that there exists at least one limit cycle for α > 0 and that there are none for α < 0. 7.3.11 (Cycle graphs) Suppose ẋ = f ( x ) is a smooth vector field on R 2 . An improved version of the Poincaré-Bendixson theorem states that if a trajectory is trapped in a compact region, then it must approach a fixed point, a closed orbit, or something exotic called a cycle graph (an invariant set containing a finite number of fixed points connected by a finite number of trajectories, all oriented either clockwise or counterclockwise). Cycle graphs are rare in practice; here’s a contrived but simple example. a)  Plot the phase portrait for the system r ˙ = r ( 1 − r 2 ) [ r 2 sin 2 θ + ( r 2 cos 2 θ − 1 ) 2 ] θ ˙ = r 2 sin 2 θ + ( r 2 cos θ − 1 ) 2 where r, θ are polar coordinates. (Hint: Note the common factor in the two equations; examine where it vanishes.) b)  Sketch x vs. t for a trajectory starting away from the unit circle. What happens as t → ∞? 7.3.12 (A heteroclinic cycle in rock-paper-scissors) The three-dimensional system P ˙ = P [ ( a R − S ) − ( a − 1 ) ( P R + R S + P S ] R ˙ = R [ ( a S − P ) − ( a − 1 ) ( P R + R S + P S ] S ˙ = S [ ( a P − R ) − ( a − 1 ) ( P R + R S + P S ] , where the parameter a > 0, is a generalization of the rock-paper-scissors model studied in Exercise 6.5.20. Previously, we studied the special case a = 1 and showed that the system had two conserved quantities, E 1 ( P , R , S ) = P + R + S , E 2 ( P , R , S ) = P R S . For a ≠ 1 it turns out that the system above has a cycle graph, or what is more commonly known nowadays as a heteroclinic cycle. With a few deft strokes, we can use the functions E 1 and E 2 to prove that a heteroclinic cycle exists (Sigmund 2010, p. 42 ). The point of this exercise is to provide a more natural instance of a cycle graph than that in Exercise 7.3.11. a)  Show that for the system above, Ė 1 = (1 − E 1 )( a −1)( PR + RS + PS ). Hence, E 1 is no longer conserved everywhere, but it is conserved if we restrict attention to the set where E 1 = 1. This set, defined by all ordered triples of real numbers (P, R, S ) such that P + R + S = 1, is invariant; any trajectory that starts on it stays on it forever. Describe this set geometrically; what simple shape is it? b)  Consider a subset of the set in (a), defined by the condition that P, R, S ≥ 0 in addition to P + R + S = 1. Show that this subset, which we’ll call T , is also invariant. What simple shape is it? From now on, we’ll restrict attention to the dynamics on the set T. c)  Show that the boundary of T consists of three fixed points connected by three trajectories, all oriented in the same sense, and hence is a cycle graph (hetero-clinic cycle). d)  Show that E ˙ 2 = ( a − 1 ) E 2 2 [ ( p − R ) 2 + ( R − S ) 2 + ( S − P ) 2 ] . e)  Using the results of parts (b)–(d), show that Ė 2 vanishes at the boundary of T and at the interior fixed point ( P * , R * , S * ) = 1 3 ( 1 , 1 , 1 ) . f)  Explain why the previous results imply that for a > 1, the interior fixed point attracts all trajectories on the interior of T. g)  Finally, show that for a < 1, the heteroclinic cycle attracts all trajectories that start in the interior of T (except, of course, for the interior fixed point itself). 7.4 Liénard Systems 7.4.1 Show that the equation ẍ + µ ( x 2 − 1) ẋ + tanh x = 0, for µ > 0, has exactly one periodic solution, and classify its stability. 7.4.2 Consider
